data.title: Parsons: More Doubts Than Answers
data.description:
In the summer I concluded that Parsons (PSN) was overcoming DOGE scares, which also followed a period in which shares had already had incredible momentum, mostly in 2024.

Following a big pullback in the share price, a modest 2025 growth outlook, buybacks, and bolt-on M&A efforts seem to be a reasonable recipe for sustainable valuation creation to continue.

In reality, both sales and orders are coming in softer than expected, and while earnings have been holding up quite well, I am surprised by the absolute share price performance, leaving me not interested here.

Technology Solutions For Various Applications

Parsons is a so-called digitally enabled solutions provider that aims to create the future of defense, intelligence, and critical infrastructure solutions. The business was founded in 1944, aiming to deliver tomorrow's solutions today.

The company employs nearly 20,000 workers, who generated nearly $7 billion in sales in 2024, some $6.8 billion to be more precise. The largest of these is a $4.0 billion Federal Solutions segment, a segment posting EBITDA margins equal to 10.4% of sales. After a softer order intake in the year, the backlog has come down a bit, yet a $4.7 billion backlog exceeded the annual revenue base by a comfortable margin.

The critical infrastructure segment is a bit smaller, with $2.7 billion in sales, these further being lower-margin businesses with EBITDA margins reported at 6.9%. An order intake of well over $3.1 billion was rather strong that year, with the backlog of that segment seen at $4.2 billion, equal to a backlog exceeding 1.5 times sales.

Products and services offered include engineered systems, defense & intelligence, and infrastructure, with such services applied to markets relating to transportation, space, cyber, infrastructure production, etc. The business is largely a US-based business, a region in which three-quarters of sales are generated, complemented by operations in Canada and the wider European region.

Parsons was just a $3 billion business in 2015, posting operating margins in the low single digits. Organic growth and M&A efforts made it so that revenues peaked at $6.8 billion in 2024, as GAAP operating margins rose to high single digits. Meanwhile, some 20% dilution has been incurred over time, to pay for M&A efforts, which in turn resulted in the growth reported, at least in part.

The business went public in 2019 and traded around the $30 mark in the first years since the offering. Increased defense spending and the issues between Russia and Ukraine sent shares up to a peak in the low $100s late in 2024. A reversal of strong momentum made shares fall to the $60 mark in the spring of 2025, only to recover to the $75 mark over the summer.

Valuation Considerations

After a strong 2023, the company grew 2024 sales by another 24% to $6.8 billion, as much of the growth was driven by organic growth. Operating profits of $428 million translated into GAAP earnings of $235 million, equal to $2.12 per share. Adjusted earnings came in at $3.26 per share, with realistic earnings seen around $2.75 per share if stock-based compensation expenses are adjusted for.

The company called for 2025 sales to move up to $7.0-$7.5 billion, with EBITDA seen up to $640-$710 million, this number comparing to a $605 million number reported in 2024. That should set the company up for a roadmap of earnings around $3 per share, yet the writing was on the wall with the 2024 backlog up just 3% to $8.9 billion. Furthermore, there was the issue of DOGE having an impact on government budgets.

Following a mere 1% increase in 2025 sales, it was evident that Parsons saw softer momentum, with organic sales down 2%, with this resetting the full-year outlook. Trying to ignite growth, Parsons announced a bolt-on deal to acquire Chesapeake Technologies in an $89 million deal.

With realistic earnings seen around $3.25 per share, and pro forma net debt seen just over a billion, shares traded around 23 times earnings at $75 per share. This made me cautious again, with a small premium in the valuation reappearing, all while organic growth was reported negative.

Valuations Come In

Following the summer, shares of Parsons have gradually risen to the $80s in the fall, and following a big setback in December, shares now trade at $70 per share, after having traded in the lower sixties in recent weeks.

In August, Parsons reported a soft second quarter with reported revenues down 5% to $1.6 billion as organic revenues were down 9%. Despite these soft topline trends, the company hiked the full-year guidance in a smaller way, after expectations were reset following the release of the first quarter results.

The autumn was filled with contract awards and a bolt-on M&A, and in November, Parsons announced as much as a 10% fall in third-quarter sales, with organic revenues down 14%. This comes as the business has seen softer momentum, as is evident in the backlog of $8.83 billion, which is up a mere $50 million from the year before. This made it so that the full-year guidance was cut across the board again.

Despite the softer momentum, year-to-date GAAP earnings are reported five cents ahead of last year at $1.69 per share, driven by some one-time costs incurred last year. Adjusted earnings year-to-date are down six cents to $2.42 per share, which excludes nearly $0.30 per share in stock-based compensation expenses. Otherwise, realistic earnings are trending closer to $3 per share (or just below that), lower than I anticipated before, driven by soft topline trends.

Net debt has come down to $815 million, perfectly manageable given that EBITDA for the year is seen at a midpoint of $615 million. With 109 million shares trading at about $70, the market value of Parsons stands at $7.6 billion, for a near $8.5 billion enterprise valuation.

With shares having fallen a bit to $70 per share, the earnings multiple remains the same at 23 times earnings. This comes in between shares having fallen a bit since the summer and my realistic earnings estimate having been cut by a quarter to $3.00 per share, at best.

And Now?

While these results are soft, shares were still trading at higher levels in the fall, but this changed in December. This came as the company saw a multi-billion dollar air traffic control order being awarded to its peer Peraton, causing shares to fall by nearly a fifth overnight. Offsetting this big headwind, the company has announced numerous contract awards and order intake in the final weeks of the year and, in fact, early in 2026.

Weighing all this, I am performing a balancing act. Of course, some order intake by year-end is welcomed, as the year-over-year decline in sales is quite severe, thereby concerning as well.

With leverage seen just over 1 time here, shares have commanded a premium valuation in the low twenty times earnings multiple. This, in between soft momentum on the topline, makes me a bit more reserved on shares for Parsons heading into 2026.

data.title: Capital Southwest: Income Still Works - Just Don't Expect The Last Cycle
data.description:
The thesis for Capital Southwest (CSWC) is about setting expectations in a falling rate environment - absorbing an expected change in total return outcomes compared to what we have been used to in the past few years. This analysis does not, therefore, dive into the business or management quality. It does not focus on dividend safety (which appears covered). The analysis tries to focus on CSWC's return-generating mechanism as the rate regime changes toward normalization. I find CSWC still a buy despite lowered expectations - it still offers mid-single-digit prospects in the base case, which is reasonable for BDC income plays in the new regime.

A Brief History of Time

With a portfolio that has a 96.6% floating rate exposure, NII and portfolio yield expansion are extremely sensitive to rates. As base rates rose rapidly in 2022, asset yields improved almost immediately from ~9% post COVID lows to ~13% in 2023. The pre-COVID rate cut normal levels were around 10-11%. Lagging funding costs also meant temporarily elevated NII - rising from ~$0.45 per share to ~$7 at peak (reflected in 2023). That those peak levels were not new steady states is already apparent, with rate cut impacts already showing up in a stabilizing yield now closer to 12%, while NII is stabilizing around $0.6 per share.

Since 2015, CSWC has shown a cumulative dividend coverage of over 110% and paid out around $4.3 per share of special or supplemental dividends. Currently, supplemental dividends are around 9-10% of the total dividend payout. The contribution of special and supplemental dividends is already trending down as CSWC braces for normalization post a peak earnings era. The earnings uplift was (as seen in portfolio yield and NII charts) extremely dependent on rate convexity (not just underwriting improvements or changes in portfolio construction).

Structural Base Likely to Persist

CSWC's defensive strength is apparent from extremely reasonable operating leverage of ~1.6% (below the industry average of 2.6-2.7%). This is trending structurally downward, which is great for breakeven asset yield and shows CSWC can tolerate earnings compression better than peers. This may not be an upside catalyst, but it shows CSWC's resilience as rates fall further.

The balance sheet posturing is also conservative - the regulatory debt-to-equity ratio is ~0.91x. A large portion of liabilities are long-dated and fixed. The overall approach has always been to keep leverage low (liquidity levels above unfunded commitments) and focus on volatility control and credibility over pure ROE optimization. This was emphasized by the management in Q1 FY2026, and again clarified in Q2.

We don't really feel like the need to reach additional leverage necessarily. All of these metrics can move around over time. But generally speaking, we're going to take a more conservative bend. Especially, we're a smaller BDC, right? We -- I think we earned our credibility in the market, but we still believe having a conservative infrastructure, having conservative leverage communicates to the market sort of the way we do business here, and we think that could probably help our price to book in the end.

we believe our balance sheet is well positioned with low leverage and significant liquidity, which allows us to continue to be active and opportunistic in all economic environments.

The credit quality also remains well supported, as reported in the latest portfolio disclosures. ~91% of investments rated internal 1 or 2, showing strong underwriting discipline. Non-accruals are ~1% of portfolio fair value, indicating that only a very small portion of assets are currently failing to service debt. More importantly, non-accruals have steadily improved from ~2.7% at the end of 2024. This comforts the Buy thesis - there is no systemic stress brewing at CSWC's portfolio level. The portfolio is heavily first lien focused. Future defaults could increase, but should remain idiosyncratic rather than correlated (unless market conditions deteriorate significantly).

Taken together, structural improvements in operating leverage, a reasonable portfolio credit condition, and a conservative leverage bias mean CSWC's downside protection seems well covered. We are not talking about drawdown scenarios that threaten solvency or dividends, but result in mild NAV erosion at worst. The question, therefore, becomes one of what kind of upside to expect. The same structure of conservative equity participation limits upside potential in my view, particularly as rates are no longer the driver they were in 2022-23. That does not weaken the buy thesis; it calls for expectation setting.

Subdued Expectations Still Reasonable

The peak to mid-cycle transition is heavily dependent on the rates trajectory, as we have seen in the past. Given the high rate of exposure, management's own indications (illustrative) point to 11-12% NII erosion if rates drop by 100 bps (and 17-18% if they drop by 150 bps). I have assumed the TTM NII levels of ~$2.4 for my % change calculations. The speed of cuts should not be a material factor in my view, given the market's discounting behavior based on terminal earnings expectations - particularly a lesser factor for longer term investors.

Current earnings do not reflect peak rates and peak spreads - as seen in the drop in NII already—but there is room for a further ~15% decline over the next couple of years. That sets expectations for quarterly NII at just above $0.5 - reflecting structural improvements in operating leverage since the 2019-20 levels of $0.4-$0.45, but acknowledging rate impacts.

CSWC has a reasonable buffer of $1.13 per share of undistributed taxable income (UTI), which could be used to defend baseline dividends if NII drops to around $0.5. And that could last quite a few quarters of marginally reduced dividend levels between $0.55 and $0.58 ($0.58 is the current base). But dividends are also looking increasingly managed. The LTM coverage has dropped from the cumulative 110% since 110 to 104%. And the ~9-10% of current dividends (shown before as special and supplemental) could definitely be cut at some point, if the macro worsens. Arguing conservatively, some cuts in base dividends could also show up, depending on how management wants to balance the UTI and payouts. Assuming $0.5 as a base for the NII, that also becomes the conservative case dividend outlook.

Valuations should also contract a bit as NII drops, with premiums shrinking if dividends are revised. CSWC is no longer a growth or rerating vehicle (no leverage push, no spread chasing anyway) - the transition to a strong income vehicle may see a further 5-10% valuation discount (depending on how much of the rate impact is already factored in). Since BDC valuations are often not about precise multiples (with NAV reported quarterly), my expectations are based on the fact that CSWC's conservative approach reduces drawdown possibilities and defends the income thesis even if capital appreciation stagnates.

At current prices, for fresh entries, a $0.5 dividend scenario (not immediately applicable) translates to a relatively healthy ~8.5% yield despite a step back. Layering in 2-4% NAV erosion due to credit pressures (and not deep because credit health appears strong), 5-10% premium erosion as CSWC enters a mid-cycle regime, a 3-year total return of 4-5% can be reasonably expected - still decent returns in what is assumed to be a lower interest regime going ahead. This implies total returns close to the lowest levels seen for a 3-year holding period in the past 10 years (barring the COVID crash). That could look overly pessimistic - but that is what I aimed to check in the thesis (a conservative outcome floor).

For total return investors, now is potentially not the best time to buy CSWC. When entries are timed well, 3-year total returns are very often north of 50%. So, waiting for better entries is advised for those looking at CSWC from a growth lens. I am looking at CSWC from a quality income lens with reduced drawdown possibilities. There, a mid single digit total return looks decent enough for a Buy on income/defensive returns perspective alone. And I see the odds of the next three years leaning towards this defensive income regime more than the outsized growth it has delivered in spurts in the past. As long as investors reset expectations from the double digit highs of 2021-25, CSWC's Buy thesis should work out very well.

data.title: RVT: Small-Cap Stocks Poised To Outperform In 2026
data.description:
(Note: This article was originally published exclusively for members of the Inside the Income Factory investing group.)

As a now retired income investor, I follow the macro events that dictate which investments may perform better at various times throughout the economic cycle. Over the past two to three years, large-cap tech stocks have been leading the way higher for US-based stocks. The Magnificent Seven (MAGS) stocks have been driving equity returns due in large part to the AI rally that has driven exponential growth in the large-cap tech sector. But now in early 2026, investors are looking to rotate out of large caps, and one area that looks promising this year is the small-cap equity asset class. According to Columbia Threadneedle Investments:

With so much attention on the “Magnificent Seven,” many investors are looking for opportunities in other parts of the market. Our forecast suggests that U.S. small-cap stocks will outperform large caps over the next several years. Two dynamics, in particular, make this an area to watch:

  • Interest rate sensitivity: Lower interest rates typically benefit small-cap companies more than their large-cap counterparts. Smaller firms often rely on borrowing to fuel growth. Lower financing rates make that debt easier to service and new projects more profitable.
  • Improved market breadth: We see improving market breadth, measured by the percentage of Russell 3000 companies trading above their 200-day moving average. With more stocks benefiting from positive momentum, we are more likely to see a rally that extends beyond just a few key mega-cap names. We believe this momentum has room to run, given the higher levels of breadth we observed in 2021.

As an income investor looking to capitalize on this macro trend, I am looking to add shares of a closed-end fund that has been around for nearly forty years and has delivered market-beating returns over the past ten years and since inception with its small-cap equity holdings. That CEF is the Royce Small-Cap Trust (RVT), initially launched in 1986 by Charles (Chuck) Royce as the Royce Value Trust. Chuck Royce will be 86 in 2026 and these days has a whole team managing the fund, which is one of several investment funds offered by Royce Investment Partners, now a Franklin Templeton company.

Over the past ten years, RVT has delivered consistently strong total returns (with dividends reinvested) and has at times beaten the S&P 500.

Although the fund benchmark is the Russell 2000 and the fund’s investment objective is to outperform the benchmark, which it did for all time periods measured as of 12/31/25 (from the fund website).

The Income Compounding Effect

One of the best ways to illustrate the power of my Income Compounder approach of reinvesting dividends (modeled after Steven Bavaria’s Income Factory) into more shares of income funds like RVT is this chart showing the growth in the value of a $10,000 investment made at fund inception, nearly 40 years ago. That investment as of 9/30/25 would be worth $479,323, and even more than that today!

Another way to look at this income compounding effect is to compare RVT performance with a fund that tracks its benchmark, iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM), which pays minimal dividends and relies almost exclusively on capital gains. As you can see, RVT delivered a total return of 255% over the past ten years compared to 190% for IWM.

RVT Fund Basics

One thing to note regarding RVT that makes it stand out as a Buy in January 2026 is the fact that it trades at a discount to NAV of -9% (despite a rising NAV). Another is the current yield shown of 13%, which is somewhat distorted due to a larger than typical fourth quarter dividend paid in 2025. Assuming the more typical quarterly dividends of roughly $0.30 per share going forward in 2026, the yield would be approximately 7% based on the closing market price of $16.74 as of 1/8/26.

The fund holdings include what Morningstar classifies as Small-Cap Core holdings, about halfway between growth and value, as of June 30, 2025, as shown on the fact sheet.

Those holdings included 467 equity positions as of 9/30/25, with top allocations to Industrials at 25% and Financials at 21% of total portfolio value, also shown on the fund fact sheet.

The top ten holdings represent less than 12% of the total portfolio, which is an indication that the portfolio is diversified and not subject to much concentration risk if, for example, something bad happened to top holding IES Holdings, Inc. (IES).

Why Small-Cap Stocks in 2026?

According to this very detailed insight into the outlook for small caps in 2026, Royce portfolio managers offer their comments, which are mostly optimistic:

Miles Lewis: We believe that both small-cap quality and value are poised for meaningful rebounds in 2026. 2025’s returns, particularly since the April lows, have been driven primarily by lower quality, speculative stocks and just about anything that is an obvious beneficiary of the AI boom … Furthermore, more “traditional” businesses models—those that have healthy margins, generate free cash flow, grow modestly, and have strong, self-funding balance sheets that also trade at attractive valuations (i.e., quality value stocks) should recapture the interest of investors as the junk rally fizzles.

Chip Skinner: We are constructive on small cap growth stocks as we head into 2026, primarily due to the accommodative fiscal and monetary policies of the current administration. The speculative activity that characterized the early part of the fourth quarter has since faded, and we are encouraged by a broadening in positive earnings revisions as macro headwinds continue to ease.

A key—and in our view underappreciated—tailwind entering 2026 is the acceleration of fiscal spending tied to onshoring initiatives, industrial policy, infrastructure, and energy-related programs. Coupled with an expected Fed easing cycle over the coming months, these forces should support meaningful economic expansion and create a favorable backdrop for small-cap growth companies. Many of these businesses have spent the past few years improving cost structures and sharpening execution, positioning them to deliver strong operating leverage as demand reaccelerates in 2026.

Kavitha Venkatraman: Regardless of one’s politics, we think the “Big Beautiful Bill” will prove highly stimulative to small-cap companies and to lower-end consumers in 2026. The 100% bonus depreciation for certain capital investments and immediate expensing of R&D spend—as opposed to it being amortized over several years—are both attractive features of the bill, particularly for smaller companies. These new rules incentivize businesses to redirect their saved tax dollars to productive uses, which is powerful for smaller businesses.

Steven McBoyle: I expect U.S. small-cap Industrials—particularly precision manufacturers, engineered components suppliers, and value-added industrial technology providers—to perform well in 2026. We are already seeing increased activity in select areas, such as improving order books across specialty manufacturing, an improved aerospace supply chain, and growth in automation/controls applications. Against this favorable backdrop, operating leverage appears poised to expand as supply chains normalize and freight and input costs stabilize.

Francis Gannon: The Russell 2000 Index is up more than 45% since the market’s low on 4/8/25, a span that has also seen small-caps beat their large-cap counterparts. In light of this dynamic performance, it may seem counterintuitive that my outlook has not shifted much since the end of the second quarter. Even with these robust results, however, small-cap stocks as a group remain far more attractively valued than their large- and mega-cap peers, as measured by our preferred index valuation metric, EV/EBIT—enterprise value over earnings before interest & taxes. The same holds true for micro-caps—which have rebounded even more impressively since early April, up more than 68%—relative to large-cap stocks.

Risks to Small-Cap Performance

One of the things that has been a tailwind for small-cap stocks in 2025 was the latest Fed rate cut and a continuing consensus belief in at least one to two additional rate cuts in 2026. If inflation starts to increase again as the impact from tariffs wears off (or the Supreme Court rules against them), the rate cut talk might be cut short and the small-cap rally will be interrupted again.

The shift from the AI-frenzied rally in growth stocks could also lose steam quickly and switch to a broader fear in the market as valuations suddenly appear excessive even for those small-cap value stocks that have been improving their balance sheets. When the tide goes out, all boats (stocks) will be affected, even the ones that have been undervalued relative to large caps that have been booming.

Conclusion: RVT is Worth Buying and Should Perform Well in 2026

The comment below from Royce Managing Director Francis Gannon sums up the current environment and expected outcome for small-cap stocks, making the case for a Buy rating on RVT, which I agree with:

So, while a fair amount of uncertainty exists in the U.S. economy and on the geopolitical front, our investment teams are highly confident that small-cap can sustain, if not build on, its nascent market leadership. Finally, I would remind investors that the opportunity still exists to build one’s small-cap allocation at attractive valuations. We continue to see the current period as an opportune time to invest in select small-caps for the long run.

In other words, even though small caps have begun to take over the leadership position from large-cap stocks in early 2026, there is still plenty of upside ahead, especially for long-term income investors who wish to compound their income by reinvesting dividends. If you do not already have an allocation to small-cap stocks in your income portfolio, consider RVT while it still trades at a wide discount to NAV.

data.title: Airship AI: Backlog Helps, But Timing Still Rules
data.description:
Q3 FY25 was another reminder of how volatile Airship AI Holdings, Inc.'s (AISP) business is. It is still primarily a procurement-driven story, one where revenue was lower year over year, cash burned faster than I would like, and the overall results depended more on contract awards and shipment timing than on the company's execution.

The stock is down another ~40% from my October article, but I don't think that is a sign for us to dismiss what is happening in the background. The company has a solid backlog, along with a large deal pipeline and recent federal awards, so it is gaining traction with the right customers. I discussed the warrant exercise in October, too.

Now, I want to assess the company's performance, whether it is any closer to being a bit more consistent, and whether the current setup justifies more patience or just caution.

Q3 2025 In Review

  • Revenue: $1.18M (vs. $2.87M YoY).

  • Gross profit: $0.60M.

  • Gross margin: ~51%.

  • Operating loss: $(2.87)M.

  • Net income: $6.4M (driven by non-cash fair value gains).

Year-to-date (FY25: The nine months ended Sept. 30):

  • Revenue: $8.83M (vs. $19.84M YoY).

  • Operating loss: $(6.60)M.

Balance sheet (end of Q3):

  • Cash: $5.76M.

  • Deferred revenue: ~$7.4M.

  • Backlog: ~$11M (shipping Q4 2025 into early 2026).

  • Validated pipeline: ~$166M (18-24 month conversion window).

I can't say that Q3 FY25 was a good-looking quarter, but after Q2's performance, I'm not surprised. The large federal purchase orders that came in Q3 FY24 didn't repeat this time, and that's a blow when most of your revenue is recognized in-quarter and depends on shipment timing.

Besides that, the gross margin was just above 50%, which is 20+ percentage points lower on both a sequential and YoY basis. Of course, I understand that the margins are lower because the company sold a heavier mix of third-party hardware, but that buttresses my point about the company still selling solutions. It cannot yet rely solely on pure software sales, which is where the higher margins are.

However, you wouldn't quite know that the results weren't great if you just looked at the net income figure of $6.4 million. The company's operating loss for Q3 came out to $2.9 million, but it also recorded $9.3 million in non-cash gains related to its warrant and earnout liabilities. That's what gave it the net income boost. It has way less cash now than it did at the beginning of FY25, so it makes sense that it needed the warrant exercise after Q3 ended.

I'm not writing it off, though, because I'm considering the size of the order book here. The company has a backlog of around $11 million, and its validated pipeline amounts to $166 million. Those figures are evidence that there's a lot of demand for what Airship is selling, so the question now is about its conversion timing and consistency.

The main issue here is that even though it has these tailwinds, delivering on them depends on when customers want their shipments and pay for their orders. It is especially true when you remember that a big part of Airship's sales outlook depends on the US federal government, which is a strategic customer.

These government apparatuses (law enforcement and security agencies have big budgets, long-term needs, and high switching costs once they deploy the systems, but they also have slow and bureaucratic sales cycles. So, it is likely that they push contract awards and shipments, and that affects how and when Airship can recognize its revenue.

I've discussed these in more depth in my previous articles, but the fact is that Airship needs to shift more of its revenue towards software, support, and analytics. That SaaS base comes with recurring revenue, which will give the stock a more solid earnings and multiple profile. Conversely, it must work hard to ensure that converting its backlog is much more predictable than the current rate.

Liquidity & Dilution

Back in October, Airship managed to raise around $9.7 million via a warrant exercise at a price of $4.50 per warrant, so it shouldn't have any cash worries for a while. You can read my full breakdown of the warrant story in the previous coverage. The dilution overhang is still there, given that the stock's current price is around $3.1/share, and now, the future upside is capped by the larger warrant base that will come into play if the stock performs well.

Still, I like that it was non-debt capital, so there are no refinancing risks, covenants, or interest payments for a company that is still trying to execute on and scale its business model. What I want to see next is progress on the revenue side of things because it can't keep relying on capital markets. The runway is longer, yes, but we need Airship to execute and deliver on its potential if it wants to avoid unnecessary future dilutions that can affect investor confidence.

The Stock Is Cheap On Paper

Airship is much cheaper today than it was when I first called it a Buy, which is great if you just want to get in at a lower price. I still believe in the business model and trajectory, but the numbers right now don't inspire confidence just yet. I don't think that any near-term valuation based on "normalized" earnings is fully grounded or justifiable. Long-term, yes, but for now, it's more aspirational, at least until the company has a steadier revenue cadence and we can see a clearer margin structure.

I already laid out my position on the proof points I want to see for Airship to justify more bullishness in the short to medium-term, so it's a waiting game for now.

A Question Of Execution And Consistency

Airship AI is selling products that solve real problems in the right end markets, and it has a big, credible pipeline, but consistency is still the big question. Dilution risk is there too, even though the valuation is cheaper than before. All the same, I can't fully underwrite the upside given the way the last two quarters have gone. I'm waiting for the next few earnings reports to come in so I can gauge Airship's earnings power before re-evaluating my stance.
